timestamps.title: Stanford Meeting
timestamps.description: Larry Page and Sergey Brin meet at Stanford, sparking their partnership.

timestamps.title: BackRub Project
timestamps.description: Larry and Sergey begin the BackRub project, developing PageRank.

timestamps.title: BackRub Demo
timestamps.description: BackRub is demoed to search engines, but deals fall through.

timestamps.title: Google Name
timestamps.description: The project is renamed Google, inspired by 'Googol'.

timestamps.title: Google Founded
timestamps.description: Google Inc. is founded after Andy Bechtolsheim writes a $100,000 check.

timestamps.title: Garage Office
timestamps.description: Google moves into Susan Wojcicki’s Menlo Park garage.

timestamps.title: Seed Funding
timestamps.description: Google raises $1 million from investors including Jeff Bezos.

timestamps.title: Venture Capital
timestamps.description: Google raises $25 million from Kleiner Perkins and Sequoia.

timestamps.title: Yahoo Deal
timestamps.description: Google signs a major deal with Yahoo to power search results.

timestamps.title: Eric Schmidt Joins
timestamps.description: Eric Schmidt becomes CEO, forming a three-person leadership team.

timestamps.title: AdWords Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches AdWords with auction and pay-per-click model.

timestamps.title: AOL Partnership
timestamps.description: Google signs a landmark ad deal with AOL, betting the company.

timestamps.title: AdSense Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches AdSense, expanding ad revenue to publisher sites.

timestamps.title: IPO
timestamps.description: Google goes public with a Dutch auction IPO, raising $1.7 billion.

timestamps.title: Google Founded
timestamps.description: Google is founded by Larry Page and Sergey Brin in Menlo Park, California.

timestamps.title: Gmail Development
timestamps.description: Paul Buchheit begins developing Gmail, pioneering web-based email and AJAX.

timestamps.title: Gmail Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches Gmail with one gigabyte of free storage and invite-only access.

timestamps.title: IPO
timestamps.description: Google goes public, raising $1.67 billion and becoming a major tech company.

timestamps.title: Maps Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches Maps, introducing dynamic web mapping and AJAX to millions.

timestamps.title: Android Acquisition
timestamps.description: Google acquires Android, setting the stage for its mobile operating system.

timestamps.title: Docs Acquisition
timestamps.description: Google acquires Writely, launching Google Docs with real-time collaboration.

timestamps.title: YouTube Acquisition
timestamps.description: Google acquires YouTube for $1.65 billion, entering the online video market.

timestamps.title: DoubleClick Acquisition
timestamps.description: Google acquires DoubleClick for $3.1 billion, expanding its ad business.

timestamps.title: Chrome Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches Chrome browser, revolutionizing web speed and security.

timestamps.title: Android Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches Android with the HTC Dream, beginning its dominance in mobile.

timestamps.title: Droid Launch
timestamps.description: Motorola Droid launches on Verizon, propelling Android to mass adoption.

timestamps.title: Google+ Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches Google+, its major social networking initiative.

timestamps.title: Alphabet Formation
timestamps.description: Google reorganizes as Alphabet, with Sundar Pichai named CEO of Google.

timestamps.title: Transformer Paper
timestamps.description: Google publishes the Transformer paper, laying the foundation for modern AI.

timestamps.title: YouTube Revenue Milestone
timestamps.description: YouTube ad revenue surpasses $36 billion, making it the world’s largest media platform.

timestamps.title: AI Vision Stated
timestamps.description: Larry Page declares Google's ultimate goal is artificial intelligence in a public statement.

timestamps.title: Language Model Work
timestamps.description: Noam Shazeer and George Herrick begin early language model research at Google, leading to 'Did You Mean'.

timestamps.title: AdSense Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches AdSense, powered by PHIL language model, expanding ad revenue.

timestamps.title: Deep Learning Arrival
timestamps.description: Sebastian Thrun joins Google, bringing deep learning expertise and launching Ground Truth for Maps.

timestamps.title: Hinton Joins Google
timestamps.description: Geoff Hinton gives a pivotal tech talk at Google, catalyzing neural network research.

timestamps.title: Google Brain Founded
timestamps.description: Google launches Google Brain, its deep learning research division led by Jeff Dean, Andrew Ng, and Greg Corrado.

timestamps.title: Cat Paper Published
timestamps.description: Google Brain publishes the 'cat paper', proving unsupervised learning on large neural networks.

timestamps.title: DNN Research Acquired
timestamps.description: Google acquires DNN Research, bringing Geoff Hinton, Ilya Sutskever, and Alex Krizhevsky into Google Brain.

timestamps.title: DeepMind Acquisition
timestamps.description: Google acquires DeepMind for $550M, adding world-class AI talent and research.

timestamps.title: AlphaGo Victory
timestamps.description: DeepMind's AlphaGo defeats world champion Lee Sedol, demonstrating AI creativity in Go.

timestamps.title: Transformer Paper
timestamps.description: Google Brain publishes 'Attention Is All You Need', introducing the Transformer architecture.

timestamps.title: BERT Model Released
timestamps.description: Google releases BERT, a major Transformer-based language model for search and NLP.

timestamps.title: Gemini Announced
timestamps.description: Google announces Gemini, its unified multimodal AI model, at Google I/O.

timestamps.title: DeepMind-Brain Merge
timestamps.description: Google merges DeepMind and Brain teams, forming Google DeepMind under Demis Hassabis.

timestamps.title: Gemini Released
timestamps.description: Google releases Gemini for public access, marking a new era in AI products.

timestamps.title: Gemini 1.5 Launch
timestamps.description: Google launches Gemini 1.5 with a 1 million token context window, expanding AI capabilities.

timestamps.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro
timestamps.description: Google launches Gemini 2.5 Pro, advancing multimodal AI performance and user adoption.

timestamps.title: Coca-Cola Invented
timestamps.description: Dr. John Pemberton creates the original formula for Coca-Cola in Atlanta.

timestamps.title: Brand Named
timestamps.description: Frank Robinson names the drink Coca-Cola and designs the iconic script logo.

timestamps.title: Ownership Consolidated
timestamps.description: Asa Candler acquires full rights to the Coca-Cola formula and brand.

timestamps.title: Company Incorporated
timestamps.description: The Coca-Cola Company is officially incorporated by Asa Candler.

timestamps.title: National Expansion
timestamps.description: Coca-Cola is sold in every U.S. state and territory.

timestamps.title: Bottling Rights Granted
timestamps.description: Exclusive bottling rights are granted to Thomas and Whitehead, enabling mass distribution.

timestamps.title: Trademark Protection
timestamps.description: Federal Trademark Act is passed and Coca-Cola registers its trademark federally.

timestamps.title: Contour Bottle Introduced
timestamps.description: The iconic Coca-Cola contour bottle is introduced to distinguish the brand.

timestamps.title: Bottler Network Grows
timestamps.description: Coca-Cola bottler network expands to over 1,200 operations across the U.S.

timestamps.title: Global Expansion
timestamps.description: Coca-Cola begins global expansion during World War II, supplying U.S. troops abroad.

timestamps.title: Diet Coke Launch
timestamps.description: Coca-Cola launches Diet Coke, its first major new brand since the original.

timestamps.title: Brand Value Milestone
timestamps.description: Coca-Cola is recognized as one of the world’s most valuable brands.

timestamps.title: Epic Founded
timestamps.description: Judith Faulkner founds Human Services Computing, later renamed Epic Systems, in Madison, Wisconsin.

timestamps.title: Rename Epic
timestamps.description: The company changes its name from Human Services Computing to Epic Systems.

timestamps.title: Launch Resolute
timestamps.description: Epic launches its billing module, integrating clinical and billing systems.

timestamps.title: EpicCare Debuts
timestamps.description: Epic releases EpicCare, the first graphical EMR application for ambulatory care.

timestamps.title: Launch MyChart
timestamps.description: Epic launches MyChart, the first integrated patient portal for accessing medical records online.

timestamps.title: EpicCare Inpatient
timestamps.description: Epic releases the inpatient version of EpicCare, covering overnight hospital stays.

timestamps.title: Kaiser Deal
timestamps.description: Epic wins a transformative contract with Kaiser Permanente, becoming the industry standard.

timestamps.title: Verona Campus Opens
timestamps.description: Epic opens its whimsical, large-scale campus in Verona, Wisconsin.

timestamps.title: HITECH Act Boost
timestamps.description: The HITECH Act accelerates EMR adoption, propelling Epic’s growth.

timestamps.title: Hit $1B Revenue
timestamps.description: Epic reaches $1 billion in annual revenue.

timestamps.title: Cosmos Launch
timestamps.description: Epic launches Cosmos, an anonymized database with hundreds of millions of patient records.

timestamps.title: Cerner Acquired
timestamps.description: Oracle acquires Cerner, Epic’s main competitor, reinforcing Epic’s market leadership.

timestamps.title: Record Revenue
timestamps.description: Epic achieves $5.7 billion in annual revenue and serves nearly all major US hospitals.

timestamps.title: Broadcast Rights Discovered
timestamps.description: BCCI realizes the value of cricket broadcast rights after negotiating with South Africa.

timestamps.title: Player Contracts Centralized
timestamps.description: BCCI introduces central contracts and pensions for Indian players, setting up control over talent.

timestamps.title: Lalit Modi Joins BCCI
timestamps.description: Lalit Modi is appointed to the BCCI board and begins commercializing Indian cricket.

timestamps.title: TV Rights Auctioned
timestamps.description: BCCI auctions TV rights for international cricket, securing a $620 million deal with Nimbus.

timestamps.title: India Wins T20 World Cup
timestamps.description: India wins the inaugural T20 World Cup, igniting national excitement for the new format.

timestamps.title: IPL Franchise Auction
timestamps.description: First IPL franchise auction is held, selling eight teams for $724 million.

timestamps.title: IPL Launches
timestamps.description: The inaugural IPL season begins, revolutionizing cricket with city-based teams and primetime matches.

timestamps.title: IPL Moves to South Africa
timestamps.description: IPL season is relocated to South Africa due to Indian general elections and security concerns.

timestamps.title: Modi Ousted from BCCI
timestamps.description: Lalit Modi is suspended from BCCI amid allegations of corruption and bid rigging.

timestamps.title: Spot Fixing Scandal
timestamps.description: Indian police bust spot fixing involving Rajasthan Royals and Chennai Super Kings, prompting reforms.

timestamps.title: Supreme Court Reforms BCCI
timestamps.description: Indian Supreme Court restructures BCCI and IPL governance after corruption scandals.

timestamps.title: Star Secures Media Rights
timestamps.description: Star India acquires IPL media rights for $2.5 billion over five years.

timestamps.title: New Teams Auctioned
timestamps.description: Gujarat Titans and Lucknow Super Giants are auctioned for $750M and $950M, respectively.

timestamps.title: Record Media Rights Deal
timestamps.description: BCCI signs $6.2 billion media rights deals for TV and streaming, split between Star and Viacom18.

timestamps.title: Tata Secures Sponsorship
timestamps.description: Tata acquires IPL presenting sponsorship for $60 million per year.

timestamps.title: Company Founded
timestamps.description: Hans Wilsdorf and Alfred Davis establish Wilsdorf & Davis in London.

timestamps.title: Rolex Brand Created
timestamps.description: Wilsdorf invents the name 'Rolex' for his wristwatches.

timestamps.title: First Chronometer
timestamps.description: Rolex receives the first wristwatch chronometer certification.

timestamps.title: Renamed Rolex
timestamps.description: Company officially becomes Rolex Watch Company Limited.

timestamps.title: Move to Geneva
timestamps.description: Rolex relocates headquarters to Geneva, Switzerland.

timestamps.title: Launch Oyster
timestamps.description: Rolex introduces the first waterproof wristwatch, the Oyster.

timestamps.title: Channel Swim Stunt
timestamps.description: Mercedes Gleitze wears Rolex Oyster during English Channel swim.

timestamps.title: Oyster Perpetual
timestamps.description: Rolex patents the self-winding perpetual rotor system.

timestamps.title: Foundation Created
timestamps.description: Hans Wilsdorf establishes Rolex’s charitable foundation.

timestamps.title: Launch Datejust
timestamps.description: Rolex debuts the Datejust, the first watch with a date window.

timestamps.title: Explorer Released
timestamps.description: Rolex launches the Explorer, inspired by Everest summit.

timestamps.title: Submariner Debut
timestamps.description: Rolex introduces the Submariner dive watch.

timestamps.title: GMT-Master Launch
timestamps.description: Rolex releases the GMT-Master for pilots.

timestamps.title: Daytona Introduced
timestamps.description: Rolex launches the Daytona chronograph.

timestamps.title: Paul Newman Craze
timestamps.description: Italian collectors spark global demand for Daytona.

timestamps.title: Aegler Acquisition
timestamps.description: Rolex acquires movement maker Aegler.

timestamps.title: Bucherer Acquisition
timestamps.description: Rolex acquires Bucherer retail chain.

timestamps.title: TSMC Founded
timestamps.description: Morris Chang founds TSMC as the world’s first pure-play semiconductor foundry in Taiwan.

timestamps.title: IPO Taiwan
timestamps.description: TSMC goes public on the Taiwan Stock Exchange, marking its first major financial milestone.

timestamps.title: IPO NYSE
timestamps.description: TSMC lists on the New York Stock Exchange, expanding its global investor base.

timestamps.title: Chang Retires
timestamps.description: Morris Chang retires as CEO after leading TSMC to global prominence.

timestamps.title: Chang Returns
timestamps.description: Morris Chang returns as CEO to guide TSMC through the mobile and cloud era.

timestamps.title: Apple Partnership
timestamps.description: TSMC secures a major deal to manufacture Apple’s custom chips, investing $9 billion in dedicated capacity.

timestamps.title: iPhone 6 Chips
timestamps.description: TSMC becomes sole supplier for Apple’s iPhone 6 chips, cementing its role in consumer electronics.

timestamps.title: Chang Final Exit
timestamps.description: Morris Chang retires for the last time, stepping down as chairman.

timestamps.title: 5nm Process
timestamps.description: TSMC launches the world’s first 5-nanometer chip manufacturing process, leading the industry.

timestamps.title: CapEx Surge
timestamps.description: TSMC announces $100 billion investment in new fabs over three years to maintain technological leadership.

timestamps.title: AI Era Leadership
timestamps.description: TSMC becomes the manufacturing superpower behind the global AI wave, powering leading-edge chips.

timestamps.title: TSMC Founded
timestamps.description: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company was founded as the world's first pure-play foundry.

timestamps.title: IPO in Taiwan
timestamps.description: TSMC went public on the Taiwan Stock Exchange, marking its first major financial milestone.

timestamps.title: Listed on NYSE
timestamps.description: TSMC became the first Taiwanese company to list on the New York Stock Exchange.

timestamps.title: Morris Chang Retires
timestamps.description: Founder Morris Chang retired as CEO for the first time.

timestamps.title: Morris Chang Returns
timestamps.description: Morris Chang returned as CEO during the financial crisis to steer TSMC through turbulent times.

timestamps.title: Apple Partnership
timestamps.description: TSMC secured a major deal with Apple to manufacture chips for the iPhone.

timestamps.title: A-Series Chips
timestamps.description: TSMC began producing Apple's A-series chips, starting with the A4.

timestamps.title: Morris Chang Retires Again
timestamps.description: Morris Chang retired as chairman, marking the end of his leadership at TSMC.

timestamps.title: Record Revenue Growth
timestamps.description: TSMC achieved 31% revenue growth, driven by demand for advanced chips.

timestamps.title: Dominance in 5nm
timestamps.description: TSMC solidified its position as the leader in 5nm chip manufacturing.

timestamps.title: Hermes Founded
timestamps.description: Thierry Hermes establishes Hermes in Paris as a harness and carriage outfitter for the nobility.

timestamps.title: Move to Faubourg
timestamps.description: Hermes relocates its workshop to 24 rue du Faubourg Saint-Honoré, its iconic headquarters.

timestamps.title: First Bag Introduced
timestamps.description: Hermes introduces the Haut à Courroies bag, precursor to the Kelly and Birkin bags.

timestamps.title: Zipper Innovation
timestamps.description: Emile Hermes brings the zipper to France after visiting the US, launching the first zippered jacket.

timestamps.title: Leadership Consolidation
timestamps.description: Emile Hermes buys out his brother and pivots the company toward handbags and travel goods.

timestamps.title: Handbag Launch
timestamps.description: Hermes launches its first modern handbag, marking its entry into the luxury handbag market.

timestamps.title: Sac à Dépêches Debut
timestamps.description: Robert Dumas redesigns the bag into the Sac à Dépêches, later known as the Kelly bag.

timestamps.title: Silk Scarf Launch
timestamps.description: Hermes introduces its iconic silk scarves, becoming a staple of French fashion.

timestamps.title: Orange Box Adopted
timestamps.description: Hermes adopts the signature orange box due to wartime material shortages.

timestamps.title: Kelly Bag Renamed
timestamps.description: The Sac à Dépêches is renamed the Kelly bag after Grace Kelly popularizes it.

timestamps.title: Jean-Louis Dumas Leads
timestamps.description: Jean-Louis Dumas becomes CEO, revitalizing Hermes and expanding globally.

timestamps.title: Birkin Bag Created
timestamps.description: Hermes launches the Birkin bag, designed in collaboration with Jane Birkin.

timestamps.title: IPO
timestamps.description: Hermes lists on the Paris Stock Exchange, selling 19% to the public while retaining family control.

timestamps.title: Arnault Takeover Attempt
timestamps.description: LVMH's Bernard Arnault attempts a takeover, prompting Hermes family to lock up shares.

timestamps.title: Family Defense Succeeds
timestamps.description: Hermes family successfully rebuffs LVMH, securing long-term family control.

timestamps.title: Craft School Founded
timestamps.description: Hermes opens École Hermès de Savoir-Faire, formalizing artisan training in France.

timestamps.title: Renaissance Founded
timestamps.description: Jim Simons founds Renaissance Technologies with initial capital from his flooring business.

timestamps.title: Axcom Created
timestamps.description: James Ax and Sandor Straus spin out Axcom for quantitative trading, with Renaissance as a major investor.

timestamps.title: Medallion Fund Launch
timestamps.description: Renaissance launches the Medallion Fund, combining quantitative models and data engineering.

timestamps.title: First Major Returns
timestamps.description: Medallion Fund achieves 77.8% gross returns, marking its breakthrough year.

timestamps.title: Fund Closed
timestamps.description: Renaissance closes Medallion Fund to new investors to preserve performance.

timestamps.title: Equities Model Built
timestamps.description: Peter Brown and Bob Mercer join Renaissance to build unified equities trading models.

timestamps.title: Tech Bubble Outperformance
timestamps.description: Medallion Fund posts 128% gross returns during the dot-com crash, proving its resilience.

timestamps.title: Outside Investors Exit
timestamps.description: Renaissance removes all outside investors from Medallion Fund, keeping it exclusive to insiders.

timestamps.title: Financial Crisis Gains
timestamps.description: Medallion Fund achieves 136% gross returns during the global financial crisis.

timestamps.title: Simons Retires
timestamps.description: Jim Simons retires and Peter Brown and Bob Mercer become co-CEOs of Renaissance.

timestamps.title: Leadership Change
timestamps.description: Bob Mercer steps down as co-CEO amid political controversy, but remains a scientist at the firm.

timestamps.title: Pandemic Outperformance
timestamps.description: Medallion Fund posts 149% gross returns during the COVID-19 market volatility.

timestamps.title: Microsoft Founded
timestamps.description: Bill Gates and Paul Allen found Microsoft in Albuquerque to develop software for microcomputers.

timestamps.title: Altair BASIC Launch
timestamps.description: Microsoft delivers its first product, BASIC interpreter for the Altair 8800.

timestamps.title: MITS Arbitration Win
timestamps.description: Microsoft wins arbitration, freeing it to license BASIC to other manufacturers.

timestamps.title: International Expansion
timestamps.description: Microsoft begins global operations, partnering with Japan’s Kay Nishi.

timestamps.title: Move to Seattle
timestamps.description: Microsoft relocates headquarters from Albuquerque to Seattle for better recruiting.

timestamps.title: IBM Partnership
timestamps.description: Microsoft partners with IBM to supply programming languages for the IBM PC.

timestamps.title: MS-DOS Deal
timestamps.description: Microsoft licenses and adapts QDOS, creating MS-DOS for IBM PCs.

timestamps.title: Windows Announced
timestamps.description: Microsoft announces Windows, its graphical operating environment.

timestamps.title: IPO
timestamps.description: Microsoft goes public, raising $45 million and becoming a major tech company.

timestamps.title: Office Suite Launch
timestamps.description: Microsoft launches Office, integrating Word, Excel, and other productivity apps.

timestamps.title: Windows 3.0 Release
timestamps.description: Windows 3.0 launches, becoming the standard for PC graphical interfaces.

timestamps.title: Windows 95 Launch
timestamps.description: Microsoft releases Windows 95, marking the mainstream adoption of PCs.

timestamps.title: Windows 95
timestamps.description: Microsoft launches Windows 95, a major consumer OS release that popularized the Start menu and GUI, driving PC adoption.

timestamps.title: Bundle Internet Explorer
timestamps.description: Bill Gates announces Internet Explorer will be free and bundled with Windows, beginning Microsoft's push into the browser market.

timestamps.title: Apple Investment
timestamps.description: Microsoft invests $150 million in Apple and agrees to make Internet Explorer the default Mac browser, stabilizing Apple financially.

timestamps.title: Hotmail Acquisition
timestamps.description: Microsoft acquires Hotmail, gaining a large consumer webmail service and early experience running web-scale online services.

timestamps.title: DOJ Lawsuit
timestamps.description: The U.S. Department of Justice files a major antitrust lawsuit against Microsoft alleging abuse of monopoly power in operating systems and browsers.

timestamps.title: Monopoly Ruling
timestamps.description: A federal judge issues a finding of fact that Microsoft holds monopoly power in the PC operating system market.

timestamps.title: Breakup Order
timestamps.description: A court orders Microsoft to be split into separate operating system and applications companies as a remedy for antitrust violations.

timestamps.title: Launch XP
timestamps.description: Microsoft ships Windows XP, unifying consumer and enterprise Windows lineages into a broadly adopted OS for users and businesses.

timestamps.title: Settlement Proposed
timestamps.description: Microsoft and the DOJ reach a proposed settlement that reverses the breakup order and outlines behavioral remedies.

timestamps.title: Settlement Approved
timestamps.description: The antitrust settlement is approved, instituting a five-year consent decree and interoperability requirements for Microsoft.

timestamps.title: Azure Incubation
timestamps.description: An internal incubation led by Ray Ozzie begins (code-named Red Dog), starting the project that later becomes Microsoft Azure.

timestamps.title: Facebook Investment
timestamps.description: Microsoft invests $240 million in Facebook and signs a commercial ad deal, strengthening ties to emerging social advertising.

timestamps.title: Yahoo Bid
timestamps.description: Microsoft attempts a high-profile acquisition of Yahoo (bids reached ~$47 billion) as a strategic push into search and ads.

timestamps.title: Launch Bing
timestamps.description: Microsoft launches Bing as its branded search engine, marking a renewed strategic focus on search and advertising.

timestamps.title: Ballmer Retirement
timestamps.description: Steve Ballmer announces his planned retirement as CEO, beginning the company’s leadership transition process.

timestamps.title: Satya CEO
timestamps.description: Satya Nadella is named CEO, ushering a strategic shift toward cloud-first, mobile-first priorities and accelerating Azure growth.

timestamps.title: Starbucks Founded
timestamps.description: Jerry Baldwin, Zev Siegl, and Gordon Bowker open the first Starbucks store in Seattle, inspired by Peet's Coffee.

timestamps.title: Schultz Joins
timestamps.description: Howard Schultz joins Starbucks as head of marketing, bringing new vision to the company.

timestamps.title: Italy Inspiration
timestamps.description: Schultz visits Milan and is inspired by Italian coffee bars, sparking the idea for Starbucks cafes.

timestamps.title: Acquires Starbucks
timestamps.description: Howard Schultz acquires Starbucks, merging it with Il Giornale and begins rapid expansion.

timestamps.title: Partner Program
timestamps.description: Starbucks introduces employee stock options and calls employees 'partners', pioneering workplace benefits.

timestamps.title: IPO
timestamps.description: Starbucks goes public, becoming the first major coffee company to IPO.

timestamps.title: First Acquisition
timestamps.description: Starbucks acquires Coffee Connection in Boston, expanding its footprint and gaining the Frappuccino trademark.

timestamps.title: Launch Frappuccino
timestamps.description: Starbucks launches the Frappuccino, its first cold blended drink, which becomes a major hit.

timestamps.title: Japan Entry
timestamps.description: Starbucks opens its first international store in Japan, marking the start of global expansion.

timestamps.title: China Entry
timestamps.description: Starbucks enters China, pioneering coffee culture in a tea-dominated market.

timestamps.title: Schultz Steps Down
timestamps.description: Howard Schultz steps down as CEO, becoming Executive Chairman as Starbucks reaches 3,500 stores.

timestamps.title: Schultz Returns
timestamps.description: Howard Schultz returns as CEO to lead a turnaround during the financial crisis.

timestamps.title: Mobile App Launch
timestamps.description: Starbucks launches its mobile app, revolutionizing ordering and payment in retail.

timestamps.title: Johnson CEO
timestamps.description: Kevin Johnson becomes CEO, succeeding Schultz and guiding Starbucks through digital transformation.

timestamps.title: China Growth
timestamps.description: Starbucks accelerates store openings in China, becoming a major revenue pillar.

timestamps.title: Schultz Interim CEO
timestamps.description: Howard Schultz returns as interim CEO to guide Starbucks through post-COVID challenges.

timestamps.title: Facebook Launches
timestamps.description: Mark Zuckerberg launches thefacebook.com at Harvard, introducing a new social network.

timestamps.title: Expansion Begins
timestamps.description: Facebook expands to other universities, quickly gaining traction across campuses.

timestamps.title: Palo Alto Move
timestamps.description: The founding team relocates to Palo Alto for the summer to work on Facebook full-time.

timestamps.title: First Funding
timestamps.description: Facebook receives its first investment from Peter Thiel, securing $500,000.

timestamps.title: Facebook Inc.
timestamps.description: Facebook incorporates as a company and transitions from a college project.

timestamps.title: Series A Funding
timestamps.description: Accel Partners leads a $12.7M Series A round, valuing Facebook at $98M.

timestamps.title: Photo Feature Launch
timestamps.description: Facebook launches photo sharing, driving massive engagement.

timestamps.title: Newsfeed Launch
timestamps.description: Facebook introduces Newsfeed, revolutionizing social media interaction.

timestamps.title: Open Registration
timestamps.description: Facebook opens registration to anyone, expanding beyond schools.

timestamps.title: Platform Launch
timestamps.description: Facebook launches its developer platform at F8, enabling third-party apps.

timestamps.title: Microsoft Partnership
timestamps.description: Microsoft invests at a $15B valuation and becomes Facebook’s ad partner.

timestamps.title: Sheryl Sandberg Joins
timestamps.description: Sheryl Sandberg joins as COO, professionalizing Facebook’s business operations.

timestamps.title: Like Button Launch
timestamps.description: Facebook launches the Like button, boosting engagement and ad targeting.

timestamps.title: IPO
timestamps.description: Facebook goes public, raising $16B in the largest tech IPO at the time.

timestamps.title: Instagram Acquisition
timestamps.description: Facebook acquires Instagram for $1B, expanding its mobile presence.

timestamps.title: WhatsApp Acquisition
timestamps.description: Facebook acquires WhatsApp for $19B, strengthening its messaging platform.

timestamps.title: Meta Rebrand
timestamps.description: Facebook rebrands as Meta, signaling a shift toward the metaverse.

timestamps.title: First Loan
timestamps.description: Ingvar Kamprad takes a 500 krona loan to start his trading business.

timestamps.title: IKEA Founded
timestamps.description: Ingvar Kamprad officially registers IKEA as a trading firm in Småland, Sweden.

timestamps.title: Catalog Launch
timestamps.description: IKEA begins regular weekly publication of its furniture catalog.

timestamps.title: First Showroom
timestamps.description: IKEA opens its first physical showroom in Älmhult, combining mail order with in-person experience.

timestamps.title: Flat-Pack Innovation
timestamps.description: IKEA introduces flat-pack furniture and self-assembly, revolutionizing cost and logistics.

timestamps.title: Stockholm Store Opens
timestamps.description: IKEA opens its flagship circular store on the outskirts of Stockholm, pioneering the modern IKEA store concept.

timestamps.title: Store Rebuild
timestamps.description: After a major fire, the Stockholm store is rebuilt, solidifying IKEA's store model.

timestamps.title: Testament Published
timestamps.description: Ingvar Kamprad writes 'The Testament of a Furniture Dealer', establishing IKEA's operating principles.

timestamps.title: US Entry
timestamps.description: IKEA opens its first US store in Plymouth Meeting, Pennsylvania.

timestamps.title: Leadership Change
timestamps.description: Ingvar Kamprad steps down as president of IKEA store operations.

timestamps.title: Hotdog Policy
timestamps.description: IKEA formalizes its 'hotdog product' policy, offering ultra-low price items in every category.

timestamps.title: China Entry
timestamps.description: IKEA enters the Chinese market, marking a major international expansion.

timestamps.title: Urban Stores
timestamps.description: IKEA launches small city stores, adapting to urbanization and changing retail trends.

timestamps.title: Founder Passes
timestamps.description: Ingvar Kamprad passes away at age 91, having shaped IKEA's legacy.

timestamps.title: Catalog Ends
timestamps.description: IKEA discontinues its iconic catalog after 70 years.

timestamps.title: Mars Founded
timestamps.description: Frank C. Mars establishes a chocolate-focused candy business in Minneapolis, beginning the Mars chocolate enterprise.

timestamps.title: Milky Way Launch
timestamps.description: Forrest Mars and Frank introduce the Milky Way nougat-and-caramel chocolate bar, driving rapid company growth.

timestamps.title: Chicago Factory
timestamps.description: Mars opens a modern Chicago factory and begins large-scale production, reaching about 20 million Milky Way bars annually.

timestamps.title: Snickers Launch
timestamps.description: Mars introduces the Snickers bar, which becomes a long-term flagship product for the company.

timestamps.title: Learned Chocolate
timestamps.description: Forrest Mars trains in Swiss chocolate factories to master chocolate production and adapts recipes for international markets.

timestamps.title: Pet Food Entry
timestamps.description: Mars diversifies by acquiring the Chappel Brothers pet food business, beginning the company's long-term expansion into pet care.

timestamps.title: M&M Production
timestamps.description: Forrest Mars and Bruce Murrie form M&M Limited and begin production of candy-coated M&M's in Newark with Hershey-supplied chocolate.

timestamps.title: M&M Buyout
timestamps.description: Forrest Mars purchases Bruce Murrie's 20% stake in M&M's, moving toward sole ownership of the brand.

timestamps.title: M&M Marketing
timestamps.description: Mars debuts animated M&M characters and the 'melts in your mouth, not in your hand' positioning, creating a lasting marketing franchise.

timestamps.title: Control Consolidated
timestamps.description: Forrest Mars completes purchases of remaining shares and gains full control of Mars Incorporated, uniting the family's businesses.

timestamps.title: Top US Candy
timestamps.description: Mars surpasses Hershey to become the largest U.S. candy company following aggressive product sizing and marketing strategies.

timestamps.title: Forrest Retires
timestamps.description: Forrest Mars retires and transfers ownership to his children, initiating the next generation of family leadership.

timestamps.title: Wrigley Acquisition
timestamps.description: Mars acquires Wm. Wrigley Jr. Company for $23 billion, substantially expanding its global confection portfolio.

timestamps.title: VCA Acquisition
timestamps.description: Mars expands into pet services by acquiring VCA, strengthening its global pet-care operations.

timestamps.title: Kind Acquisition
timestamps.description: Mars acquires Kind to broaden its healthy-snack portfolio and accelerate global snacking expansion.

timestamps.title: Revenue $50B
timestamps.description: Mars surpasses $50 billion in annual sales, reflecting extensive diversification across candy, pet care, and snacks.
